description: Add MCP tools for debugging the Chrome extension internals: comprehensive state inspection, internal log collection, side panel DOM/state visibility, adapter injection diagnostics, and soft reconnection. These tools give an AI agent full observability into the extension without requiring manual DevTools inspection.

userStories.title: Add internal log collector to background and offscreen scripts
userStories.description: As a developer debugging the extension, I want internal logs from the background script and offscreen document to be captured in a ring buffer so that the extension_get_logs tool (US-003) can return them without requiring manual DevTools access.
acceptanceCriteria: New file platform/browser-extension/src/log-collector.ts exports a singleton LogCollector class

LogCollector wraps console.log, console.warn, console.error, and console.info — intercepting calls, storing entries in a ring buffer, AND still calling the original console methods so DevTools output is not lost
Each log entry stores: timestamp (ms), level ('log' | 'warn' | 'error' | 'info'), message (formatted string), and source ('background' | 'offscreen' | 'side-panel')
Ring buffer holds a configurable max entries (default 500) — oldest entries are dropped when full
LogCollector provides getEntries(options) method: optional filters for level, source, since (timestamp), and limit; returns entries newest-first
LogCollector provides clear() method to flush the buffer
LogCollector provides getStats() returning { totalCaptured, bufferSize, oldestTimestamp, newestTimestamp }
Background script (background.ts) imports and initializes the log collector at startup with source='background'
Offscreen document (offscreen/index.ts) imports and initializes its own instance with source='offscreen'
Log entries are serializable (no circular references, functions, or DOM nodes — all arguments are stringified)
Individual log messages are truncated at 2000 characters to prevent memory issues from large object dumps
bun run build passes
bun run type-check passes
bun run lint passes
bun run knip passes
bun run test passes
bun run test:e2e passes

userStories.title: Add extension_get_state tool for comprehensive state inspection
userStories.description: As an AI agent debugging the extension, I want a single tool that returns the complete internal state of the extension so that I can quickly understand the overall health: WebSocket connection, all plugins with their tab states, adapter injection status, active network captures, rate limiter stats, and pending operations.
acceptanceCriteria: New file platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/extension-get-state.ts defines the extension_get_state tool using defineBrowserTool
Tool accepts no required parameters (just tabId-free — it inspects the extension itself, not a tab)
Tool dispatches to extension via 'extension.getState' method
Extension handler in browser-commands.ts implements handleExtensionGetState
Handler collects state from multiple sources and returns a comprehensive state object
Returned state includes connection: { wsConnected (boolean), mcpServerUrl (string from chrome.storage.local) }
Returned state includes plugins: array of { name, version, displayName, trustTier, urlPatterns, toolCount, tabState (from lastKnownState map), matchingTabId, matchingTabUrl }
Returned state includes networkCaptures: array of { tabId, requestCount, isCapturing } for all active capture sessions
Returned state includes offscreen: { exists (boolean) } — checked via chrome.runtime.getContexts
Returned state includes storage: { pluginCount (number of entries in plugins_meta) }
'extension.getState' added to dispatch table in message-router.ts
'extension.getState' added to ALLOWED_METHODS in offscreen/index.ts
Tool registered in platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/index.ts
Tool description explains it returns the complete internal state of the extension for debugging — connection health, plugin status, tab states, and active operations
bun run build passes
bun run type-check passes
bun run lint passes
bun run knip passes
bun run test passes
bun run test:e2e passes
userStories.notes:
This handler runs in the background script context, so it has direct access to in-memory state. Key data sources:

1. **Connection**: Read `wsConnected` flag from background.ts. Need to export it or provide a getter. Read mcpServerUrl from chrome.storage.local.

2. **Plugins**: Call `getAllPluginMeta()` from plugin-storage.ts. For tab state, import the `lastKnownState` Map from tab-state.ts (need to export it or add a getter like `getLastKnownStates()`).

3. **Network captures**: Import `isCapturing` and add a new export `getActiveCaptureTabIds()` from network-capture.ts that returns the keys of the captures Map. Also add `getCaptureStats(tabId)` that returns request count.

4. **Offscreen document**: Use `chrome.runtime.getContexts({ contextTypes: ['OFFSCREEN_DOCUMENT'] })` (Chrome 116+) to check if the offscreen document exists.

Exporting internal state requires adding a few getter functions to existing modules. Keep exports minimal — just what's needed for the state dump. Don't expose the raw Maps/state objects; return plain serializable objects.

The tool doesn't need a tabId parameter since it inspects the extension itself. Use `defineBrowserTool` with an empty or minimal input schema.

Files to create/edit: (1) Create platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/extension-get-state.ts, (2) Add to browser-tools/index.ts, (3) Add handleExtensionGetState to browser-commands.ts, (4) Add 'extension.getState' to message-router.ts dispatch table + ALLOWED_METHODS, (5) Add state getter exports to tab-state.ts, network-capture.ts, and possibly background.ts.

userStories.title: Add extension_get_logs tool for internal log retrieval
userStories.description: As an AI agent debugging extension issues, I want to retrieve internal logs from the background script and offscreen document so that I can see error messages, WebSocket events, plugin injection warnings, and other diagnostic output without opening DevTools.
acceptanceCriteria: New file platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/extension-get-logs.ts defines the extension_get_logs tool using defineBrowserTool

Tool accepts optional parameters: level (filter by 'log' | 'warn' | 'error' | 'info' | 'all'), source (filter by 'background' | 'offscreen' | 'all'), limit (max entries, defaults to 100), since (timestamp, only return entries after this time)
Tool dispatches to extension via 'extension.getLogs' method
Extension handler combines logs from background LogCollector and offscreen LogCollector (via bg:getLogs message to offscreen)
Returns { entries: Array<{ timestamp, level, source, message }>, stats: { totalBackground, totalOffscreen, bufferSize } }
Entries are sorted by timestamp descending (newest first)
When source='all' (default), merges and interleaves entries from both sources by timestamp
'extension.getLogs' added to dispatch table in message-router.ts
'extension.getLogs' added to ALLOWED_METHODS in offscreen/index.ts
Tool registered in platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/index.ts
Tool description explains it returns internal extension logs (background script + offscreen WebSocket document) and suggests filtering by level='error' to quickly find problems
bun run build passes
bun run type-check passes
bun run lint passes
bun run knip passes
bun run test passes
bun run test:e2e passes
userStories.notes:
Depends on US-001 (log collector). The handler needs to:

1. Get background logs directly from the local LogCollector instance
2. Get offscreen logs by sending a chrome.runtime.sendMessage with type 'bg:getLogs' to the offscreen document and awaiting the response
3. Merge the two arrays by timestamp
4. Apply filters (level, source, since, limit)
5. Return the result

For the offscreen message, the offscreen handler (added in US-001) should respond with its entries. Use the existing message passing pattern — send message from background, offscreen responds via sendResponse.

Actually, background → offscreen communication uses chrome.runtime.sendMessage which the offscreen listens for. But the offscreen is not a content script, so we need to check how messages flow. Looking at the existing code: the background sends messages to offscreen via chrome.runtime.sendMessage and the offscreen has a chrome.runtime.onMessage listener. Add a case for 'bg:getLogs' that calls sendResponse with the offscreen collector entries.

Files to create/edit: (1) Create platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/extension-get-logs.ts, (2) Add to browser-tools/index.ts, (3) Add handleExtensionGetLogs to browser-commands.ts, (4) Add 'extension.getLogs' to message-router.ts + ALLOWED_METHODS.

userStories.title: Add extension_get_side_panel_state tool for side panel inspection
userStories.description: As an AI agent debugging the side panel display, I want to see the side panel's current React state and rendered HTML so that I can understand what the user sees: which plugins are shown, their tab states, connection status, error messages, and overall layout.
acceptanceCriteria: Side panel bridge (bridge.ts) adds a handler for incoming 'sp:getState' messages from the background script
Handler collects the side panel's current state and DOM, then sends response back via chrome.runtime.sendMessage
Response includes rendered HTML: document.getElementById('root').innerHTML (truncated to 50000 chars)

Response includes React state snapshot: { connected, loading, pluginCount, plugins (array of { name, tabState, toolCount, enabledToolCount, activeToolCount }), versionMismatch, errors (any visible error text) }
New file platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/extension-get-side-panel.ts defines the extension_get_side_panel tool
Tool dispatches to extension via 'extension.getSidePanel' method
Extension handler in browser-commands.ts implements handleExtensionGetSidePanel
Handler sends 'sp:getState' message to the side panel via chrome.runtime.sendMessage and waits for response
If the side panel is not open (no response within 3 seconds), returns { open: false } with a message explaining the side panel is closed
If the side panel responds, returns { open: true, state: {...}, html: '...' }
'extension.getSidePanel' added to dispatch table + ALLOWED_METHODS
Tool registered in browser-tools/index.ts
Tool description explains it inspects the side panel's current display and state — useful for verifying what the user sees
bun run build passes
bun run type-check passes
bun run lint passes
bun run knip passes
bun run test passes
bun run test:e2e passes
userStories.notes:
The side panel runs in a separate extension page context. Communication between background and side panel uses chrome.runtime.sendMessage / chrome.runtime.onMessage.

The side panel already has a message listener in App.tsx that handles 'sp:connectionState' and 'sp:serverMessage'. Add a new case for 'sp:getState' that:

1. Collects the current React state (connected, plugins, loading, activeTools, versionMismatch)
2. Gets the rendered HTML from document.getElementById('root')?.innerHTML
3. Sends response back via sendResponse (return true from the listener to indicate async response)

The tricky part: the React state is inside the App component's useState hooks, not globally accessible. Options:
(a) Store state in a module-level variable that App.tsx updates (simplest)
(b) Use a ref that's accessible from outside the component
(c) Emit the state via a global event

Option (a) is simplest: in App.tsx, add a module-level `let currentState: SidePanelState | null = null;` that the component updates on every render (or via useEffect). Then the message handler can read it.

Alternatively, the message handler can be inside the App component via useEffect that registers the chrome.runtime.onMessage listener with closure access to state. The existing listener in App.tsx already has access to state — just add the 'sp:getState' case there.

Actually looking at App.tsx more carefully — the chrome.runtime.onMessage listener is already inside useEffect with access to all state variables (connected, plugins, activeTools, etc.). Add the 'sp:getState' handler there, call sendResponse with the state + HTML, return true.

For the background handler: send 'sp:getState' to the side panel via chrome.runtime.sendMessage. If the side panel is open, it responds. If not, the message goes nowhere and we timeout. Use a Promise with a 3-second timeout.

Files to create/edit: (1) Edit side-panel/App.tsx — add 'sp:getState' handler to the existing onMessage listener, (2) Add types for the new message, (3) Create browser-tools/extension-get-side-panel.ts, (4) Add handler to browser-commands.ts, (5) Add to message-router.ts + ALLOWED_METHODS + index.ts.

userStories.title: Add extension_check_adapter tool for plugin injection diagnostics
userStories.description: As an AI agent debugging why a plugin's tools are failing, I want to check the adapter injection status for a specific plugin across all matching tabs so that I can see whether the adapter is injected, whether the hash matches, whether isReady() passes, and what tools are registered in each tab.
acceptanceCriteria: New file platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/extension-check-adapter.ts defines the extension_check_adapter tool
Tool accepts plugin (required — plugin name string)
Tool dispatches to extension via 'extension.checkAdapter' method
Extension handler in browser-commands.ts implements handleExtensionCheckAdapter
Handler finds all tabs matching the plugin's URL patterns (using findAllMatchingTabs or chrome.tabs.query)
For each matching tab, handler runs chrome.scripting.executeScript in MAIN world to check globalThis.__openTabs.adapters[pluginName]

Per-tab check returns: { tabId, tabUrl, tabTitle, adapterPresent (boolean), adapterHash (string or null), hashMatch (boolean — compares adapter hash to expected from plugin metadata), isReady (boolean — calls adapter.isReady() with 5s timeout), toolCount (number of tools on the adapter), toolNames (array of tool name strings) }
Returns { plugin: name, expectedHash, matchingTabs: [...per-tab results...] }
If plugin name is not found in stored metadata, returns clear error message
If no matching tabs found, returns empty matchingTabs array with explanation
isReady() probe has 5-second timeout per tab (consistent with IS_READY_TIMEOUT_MS) — timeout results in isReady=false with a note
'extension.checkAdapter' added to dispatch table + ALLOWED_METHODS
Tool registered in browser-tools/index.ts
Tool description explains it performs deep adapter injection diagnostics and is useful when a plugin shows as ready but tools fail, or when tab state seems wrong
bun run build passes
bun run type-check passes
bun run lint passes
bun run knip passes
bun run test passes
bun run test:e2e passes

userStories.title: Add extension_force_reconnect tool for soft WebSocket recovery
userStories.description: As an AI agent diagnosing connection issues, I want to force the WebSocket to disconnect and reconnect without reloading the entire extension, so that I can recover from stale connections while preserving plugin injection state and side panel UI.
acceptanceCriteria: New file platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/extension-force-reconnect.ts defines the extension_force_reconnect tool
Tool accepts no required parameters
Tool dispatches to extension via 'extension.forceReconnect' method
Extension handler in browser-commands.ts implements handleExtensionForceReconnect
Handler sends response immediately (so the MCP server gets confirmation before the WebSocket drops)
Handler then sends a message to the offscreen document telling it to force-reconnect
Offscreen document handles new 'bg:forceReconnect' message by: closing the current WebSocket (if open), resetting backoff to initial value, and calling connect() immediately
After reconnection, the normal sync.full flow resumes (server sends plugin state, extension re-syncs tabs)
This is softer than extension_reload: it preserves injected adapters in tabs, side panel state, and plugin storage — only the server connection is reset
'extension.forceReconnect' added to dispatch table + ALLOWED_METHODS
Tool registered in browser-tools/index.ts

Tool description explains it forces a WebSocket reconnection without reloading the extension — useful when the connection is in a bad state (zombie connection, stale ping) but adapter injection and side panel should be preserved
'bg:forceReconnect' message type added to types.ts
bun run build passes
bun run type-check passes
bun run lint passes
bun run knip passes
bun run test passes
bun run test:e2e passes
userStories.notes:
The force reconnect flow:

1. MCP server dispatches 'extension.forceReconnect' via WebSocket
2. Offscreen document forwards to background (via existing message routing)
3. Background handler:
   a. Sends JSON-RPC success response back to server (important: do this FIRST before the WebSocket drops)
   b. Then sends 'bg:forceReconnect' to offscreen document via chrome.runtime.sendMessage
4. Offscreen document handler:
   a. If ws is open, calls ws.close()
   b. Resets backoffMs to initial value (1000)
   c. Calls connect() to start a fresh connection attempt

Note the ordering matters: the success response must be sent BEFORE we close the WebSocket, otherwise the response never reaches the server. Use a small setTimeout (100ms) before sending the reconnect message to the offscreen, similar to how extension_reload handles the response-then-action pattern.

In the offscreen document, add a handler in the existing chrome.runtime.onMessage listener for 'bg:forceReconnect'. This handler should:
- Set `connecting = false` (clear any in-progress connection flag)
- Close existing ws: `if (ws) { ws.close(); ws = null; }`
- Reset backoff: `backoffMs = 1000`
- Call `connect()` after a short delay

Files to create/edit: (1) Create browser-tools/extension-force-reconnect.ts, (2) Add handler to browser-commands.ts, (3) Add to message-router.ts + ALLOWED_METHODS + index.ts, (4) Add 'bg:forceReconnect' handler to offscreen/index.ts, (5) Add message type to types.ts.

userStories.title: Add E2E tests for extension debugging tools
userStories.description: As a developer, I want E2E tests for the extension debugging tools so that state inspection, log retrieval, side panel inspection, adapter diagnostics, and force reconnect are all verified through the full MCP pipeline.
acceptanceCriteria: New test describe block 'Extension debugging tools' in browser-tools.e2e.ts
Test: extension_get_state returns comprehensive state — verifies response has connection.wsConnected=true, plugins array with e2e-test plugin, and offscreen.exists=true
Test: extension_get_state shows correct tab state after opening test app tab — opens tab matching e2e-test plugin URL pattern, waits for ready state, verifies the plugin's tabState in the state dump
Test: extension_get_logs returns log entries from background — verifies entries array is non-empty and entries have timestamp, level, source, message fields
Test: extension_get_logs with level='error' filter returns only error entries (or empty if no errors)
Test: extension_get_side_panel returns { open: false } when side panel is not open

Test: extension_check_adapter returns adapter diagnostics for e2e-test plugin — opens test app tab, waits for ready, calls check_adapter, verifies adapterPresent=true, isReady=true, toolNames includes known tools
Test: extension_check_adapter returns empty matchingTabs when no matching tab is open
Test: extension_check_adapter returns error for non-existent plugin name

Test: extension_force_reconnect triggers reconnection — calls force_reconnect, waits for extension to reconnect (poll extension connected via health endpoint), verifies MCP tools still work after reconnection
BROWSER_TOOL_NAMES in helpers.ts updated to include all new tool names: 'extension_get_state', 'extension_get_logs', 'extension_get_side_panel', 'extension_check_adapter', 'extension_force_reconnect'
bun run build passes
bun run type-check passes
bun run lint passes
bun run knip passes
bun run test passes
bun run test:e2e passes
userStories.notes:
The e2e-test plugin is already set up in the fixtures and matches the test server's URL pattern. Use setupToolTest or the standard fixture pattern to get a connected extension with the e2e-test plugin registered.

For extension_get_state: this is a parameterless tool — just call it and verify the response shape. The e2e-test plugin should appear in the plugins array.

For extension_get_logs: the background script will have produced logs during startup and connection. Filter for source='background' to verify background logs are captured.

For extension_get_side_panel: in the E2E test environment, the side panel is NOT open (Playwright doesn't open the side panel by default). So expect { open: false }. Testing the open=true case would require Playwright to open the side panel, which is complex — skip for now.

For extension_check_adapter: open a tab to the test server URL (which matches the e2e-test plugin), wait for the adapter to be injected (use waitForToolResult or poll), then call check_adapter. Verify the response has the expected adapter properties.

For extension_force_reconnect: call force_reconnect, then poll waitForExtensionConnected to verify the extension reconnects. Then call a browser tool to verify the connection works.

All new tool names must be added to BROWSER_TOOL_NAMES in helpers.ts.
